text: The proposal of this video is to present the ureco-reminent capillary abnormalities with a robotic treatment and partial cystectomy under the TENT technique. The ureco is a fibrosis remnant of the allantoids, which form the median umbilical ligament as the ceiling occurs. Ureco remnant can be found during surgical treatment of infiltrate endometriosis and other surgical gynecologic pathologies. Some abnormalities can occur with the sealing of the channel during the fetal development, leading to symptoms such as umbilical drainage, chronic pelvic pain and urinary tract infections. And there are at least four specific types of uricle abnormalities. The patent uricles, uricle sinus, diverticulum and uricle cysts.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm technique to initiate the robotic procedure, exposing the reticulospase and urechial pattern for safe resection. The bladder is filled with 300 cc of methylene blue solution for enhanced anatomical exposure.

The surgeon inverts the optic 30 degrees upwards to enhance anatomical exposure for the resection of the urachal vesicle diverticulum. The procedure involves resecting the patent urachus and performing a diverticulectomy and apical cystectomy without opening the bladder mucosa, aiming for better recovery without a bladder catheter.

The surgeon uses a V-locked suture with a 2.5 cm needle to reconstruct the peritoneal defect. After treating the patent uricus located between the transverse fascia and peritoneum, the optics are repositioned 30 degrees downward to begin treating the vesicle urico diverticulum. The surgical margins are exhibited, allowing dissection of at least one centimeter of bladder mucosa free of disease. A Vicro II-O suture is used to ligate the diverticulum base in the bladder without transfixing the mucosa.

The surgeon performs a cystectomy using the tent technique, resecting the bladder component without exposing the intravesical mucosa. The detrusor muscle is re-approximated using a Vicryl-Trio with SH-II.V needle in an extra-mucosal continuous suture. The optics are positioned thirty degrees up, and the resection of two uricle cysts is demonstrated, with the first cyst located at the anterior abdominal wall, previously infected and treated with antibiotics.

The surgeon treats a second urachal cyst near the bladder dome using the tent technique. Methylene blue is used to distend the bladder, aiding in the evaluation of mucosal integrity and differentiation from the detrusor muscle. The tent technique involves upward traction of the bladder defect, followed by microdissection with monopolar energy in the detrusor muscle until reaching the vesicle mucosa, allowing ligation with generous margins without opening the bladder.

Note that the use of this technology, how easy is to perform the bladder surgical margin in the treatment of the uric abnormalities without the need to open the bladder, thus reducing the surgical time and morbidity. With this, we hope to have managed to show how reproductible is to treat effectively and safely the abnormalities of the uricles. And when necessary, using the tent technique for the treatment of the uricle vesicle defect as shown. Thank you very much.
